title: recommendedEffort returns effort=7 for unknown hardware concurrency (hwc=0), but hwc=0 means unknown not zero-core
description: When hwConcurrency is not passed and navigator.hardwareConcurrency is unavailable, hwc defaults to 0 (line 122). The guard `hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2` is then false, so the function returns 7 (highest effort). A device with unknown concurrency is likely low-end or headless, yet it receives the heaviest encode effort. The conservative choice for unknown hwc should be the lower value (6), consistent with how recommendedQualitySearch treats unknown hwc (falls into the slow-device fast-path via `hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2` being false — but that returns 'full' which is the aggressive path, making the two functions inconsistent with each other for the unknown-hwc case).
code_excerpt:
const hwc = hwConcurrency ?? (typeof navigator !== "undefined" ? navigator.hardwareConcurrency ?? 0 : 0);
  return hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2 ? 6 : 7; // MT tier on a 2-core device: don't pay effort-7 (CAP-7)

title: recommendedQualitySearch returns 'full' for MT tiers with unknown hardware concurrency (hwc=0), inconsistent with effort heuristic intent
description: When hwc is 0 (unknown), `hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2` is false, so an MT-tier device with unknown concurrency gets 'full' quality search. This contradicts the conservative intent: an unknown-concurrency device should not be assumed high-performance. The effort function treats unknown hwc as high-end (effort=7); the quality function also treats it as high-end ('full'). The two are internally consistent but both assume unknown=high-end, which is wrong for environments where hardwareConcurrency is unavailable (often constrained environments or older browsers).

title: isSimdProbe and isRelaxedProbe fingerprints could collide if probe bytes are reordered in future
description: isSimdProbe checks only `view.includes(0xfd) && view.includes(0x0f)`. isRelaxedProbe checks `view.includes(0xfd) && view.includes(0x80) && view.includes(0x02)`. The two share the `0xfd` byte. The stubs are checked in order (thread → relaxed → simd) which prevents misclassification today because relaxed probe does not contain `0x0f`. However if PROBE_RELAXED_BYTES were ever updated to include an opcode with `0x0f` in its encoding, the simd check order being last would still be safe, but if PROBE_SIMD_BYTES ever gained `0x80` and `0x02`, it would be misidentified as relaxed. The detection is fragile byte-scanning rather than comparing the exact magic sequences from PROBE_*_BYTES constants.

title: installProbeStubs stub never returns true for wasmExceptions probe, silently leaving wasmExceptions=false in all matrix tests
description: The PROBE_EH_BYTES exception-handling probe does not match any of isThreadProbe, isRelaxedProbe, or isSimdProbe (it contains no 0xfd, 0xfe, 0x80, 0x02, or 0x0f bytes in the body). The stub's final `return false` always fires for the EH probe. Since none of the matrix tests assert on caps.wasmExceptions, the wasmExceptions capability is untested across all tier combinations. A wasmExceptions=true case is never exercised.

title: beforeEach does not delete stale SharedArrayBuffer stub when originalSharedArrayBuffer was undefined
description: afterEach correctly deletes the SAB stub when originalSharedArrayBuffer is undefined (line 100-101: `else { delete (globalAny as any).SharedArrayBuffer; }`). But beforeEach (line 83-85) only restores SAB if it was originally present — it never deletes a stub left by a prior test. If a test calls setSAB(true) and then throws before afterEach can clean up, the next test's beforeEach will leave SAB in the stubbed state. The Node test runner normally runs afterEach even on failure, so in practice this is benign, but the asymmetry is a latent isolation defect.

title: detectTier has no assertion that its output is consistent with computeCapabilities selectedWasmBuild
description: detectTier() and computeCapabilities() both derive the tier independently using identical logic, but there is no runtime or test-time assertion that `caps.selectedWasmBuild === detectTier()` for all probe combinations. A future change to one path without updating the other would go undetected. Adding a single deepEqual assertion in the matrix test harness after each freshCapsAndTier() call would catch divergence immediately.

category: no property-based testing on math/state-heavy code
title: canUseThreadedWasm is only tested with three of four boolean combinations
description: The test at tier.test.ts:11-16 exercises (true,true), (true,false), (false,true), (false,false). All four combinations are actually covered — this is a non-issue. However, canUseThreadedWasm(false, false) is tested in tier.test.ts but not exercised in the matrix test canUseThreadedWasm test (tier-matrix.test.ts:176-179) which only checks three combinations. The missing (false,false) case in the matrix test is a minor coverage gap.
